defs.def: Angular velocity - The rate at which an object rotates about an axis, measured in radians per second (rad/s) and denoted by ω.
Velocity relation - The equation ω = v/R, which relates the tangential (linear) speed v of a point on a rotating body to its angular velocity ω and the radius R of its circular path.
Point mass - An idealized object with mass but no spatial extent, used to simplify analysis by ignoring rotation and internal structure.
Rigid body - An idealized solid object in which the distance between every pair of points remains constant, so it can rotate as well as translate.
Tangent - A straight line that touches a curve at exactly one point and points in the direction the curve is heading at that point.
Radially - In the direction pointing toward or away from the center of a circular path.
Tangentially - In the direction along the instantaneous velocity of an object moving on a curved path, i.e. perpendicular to the radial direction.
Normal - Perpendicular to a surface or line at a given point.
Positive work - Work done on an object when the applied force has a component in the same direction as the object's displacement, resulting in an increase in the object's kinetic energy.
Constraint force - A force exerted by a surface, track, or other boundary that restricts an object's motion to a particular path.
Kinetic energy (KE) - The energy an object possesses due to its motion, equal to ½mv² for an object of mass m moving at speed v.

question.answer:
The speed stays the same if we model the car as a point mass. A small slowdown is possible if modeled as a rigid body.
A ball on a string spins faster when pulled inward. The same principle applies when the car transitions to a tighter radius. The string does work because, as the ball spirals inward, its velocity has a radial component aligned with the tension.
The fixed frictionless track exerts only a force perpendicular to the car's velocity, so unlike the string it does no work. The curvature changes continuously along the connecting track, so the constraint force direction rotates.
This rotation means the force cannot remain perfectly perpendicular to velocity. The car's velocity is tangent to the track at every point. The constraint force is normal to the track, hence perpendicular to velocity.
At every instant, the force and velocity rotate together, maintaining perpendicularity even as curvature changes. On a changing-curvature path, the constraint force adjusts in both magnitude and direction. The centripetal acceleration behaves differently on the connecting track than it does on either circular section.
Therefore, you should reject . These facts are true but don't refute Debater A Response 2 Claim 2. Imagine you're in the car. Velocity points out the windshield, the track pushes in through your side doors. Although you're turning, your doors stay exactly 90 degrees from your windshield. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
The connecting track provides a radially inward force that accelerates the car tangentially. Therefore, you should reject .

The speed increases whether we model the car as a point mass or as a rigid body.
On a frictionless track, the rails' constraint force does no work, so kinetic energy (KE) is conserved and speed cannot increase. If true that the rails do no work, then a point-mass car would not lose speed on the smaller track.
But if KE is conserved (Debater A Argument 1 Claim 1) and the rails do no work, then there should be no slowdown on a frictionless track for a rigid body model, contra Debater A Opening. A rigid body has both translational and rotational KE; a point mass has only translational.
On a tighter track, assuming the body is constrained to yaw with the track curvature, rotational KE increases at the expense of translational KE. The rail forces that steer the rigid body do positive work, supplying the rotational KE directly; it need not come from translation.
Since ω=v/R, any drop in v also reduces ω, so rotational KE doesn't necessarily increase. ω = v/R rising or falling depends on which changes more. Energy conservation gives v₂ = v₁·(R₂/R₁)·√((I+mR₁²)/(I+mR₂²)). Pick any m, I, R₁>R₂. The formula guarantees v₂/v₁ > R₂/R₁, so ω=v/R always rises.
Therefore, you should reject . ω = v/R is a point-mass relationship. For a rigid body the center of mass doesn't follow the rail radius, so point-mass relationships don't apply. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
There is no mechanism on a fixed frictionless track to add energy to the car. Therefore, you should reject .
